title: Λέων καὶ ὄναγρος
titleEn: The Lion and the Wild Ass (the Lion's Share)
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 207 (= Perry 339). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Θήρας ἐθήρευον λέων καὶ ὄναγρος, ὁ μὲν λέων διὰ τῆς δυνάμεως, ὁ δὲ ὄναγρος διὰ τῆς ἐν ποσὶ ταχύτητος.
sentences.tr: Thēras ethēreuon leōn kai onagros, ho men leōn dia tēs dynameōs, ho de onagros dia tēs en posi tachytētos.
sentences.l: Prey were-hunting a lion and a wild-ass, the lion through his strength, the wild-ass through the in-feet swiftness.
sentences.i: A lion and a wild ass hunted game together — the lion by his strength, the wild ass by his speed of foot.
sentences.n: θήρας ἐθήρευον — 'hunted huntings': the cognate object Greek loves. The partnership's division of labor is set out in mirrored διά-phrases; the division of the spoils will not be so even.

sentences.t: Ἐπεὶ δὲ ζῷά τινα ἐθήρευσαν, ὁ λέων μερίζει καὶ τίθησι τρεῖς μοίρας, καί· « Τὴν μὲν μίαν, εἶπεν, λήψομαι ὡς πρῶτος· βασιλεὺς γάρ εἰμι· τὴν δὲ δευτέραν, ὡς ἐξ ἴσου κοινωνός· ἡ δὲ τρίτη μοῖρα αὕτη κακὸν μέγα σοι ποιήσει, εἰ μὴ θελήσεις φυγεῖν. »
sentences.tr: Epei de zōia tina ethēreusan, ho leōn merizei kai tithēsi treis moiras, kai: « Tēn men mian, eipen, lēpsomai hōs prōtos: basileus gar eimi: tēn de deuteran, hōs ex isou koinōnos: hē de tritē moira hautē kakon mega soi poiēsei, ei mē thelēseis phygein. »
sentences.l: And when animals some they-had-caught, the lion divides and sets-out three portions, and: « The one, he-said, I-shall-take as first — for king I-am; the second, as equal partner; but this third portion will-do you great harm, unless you-choose to-flee. »
sentences.i: When they had taken some game, the lion divided it into three portions and said: « The first I take because I rank first — I am king. The second, as your equal partner. And as for the third — it will do you great harm, unless you decide to run. »
sentences.n: The narrative jumps into the present tense (μερίζει, τίθησι) as the shares hit the ground — the historical present at the decisive moment; you'll see it again. The three ὡς-clauses are a parody of legal reasoning, each share with its 'title': as king, as partner, as threat.

sentences.t: Ὅτι καλὸν ἑαυτὸν μετρεῖν ἐν πᾶσι κατὰ τὴν ἑαυτοῦ ἰσχὺν καὶ δυνατωτέροις ἑαυτοῦ μὴ συνάπτειν μηδὲ κοινωνεῖν.
sentences.tr: Hoti kalon heauton metrein en pasi kata tēn heautou ischyn kai dynatōterois heautou mē synaptein mēde koinōnein.
sentences.l: That good-it-is oneself to-measure in all-things according-to the of-oneself strength, and with-those-mightier than-oneself not to-join nor to-partner.
sentences.i: Measure yourself in everything by your own strength, and do not ally or go shares with those mightier than you.
sentences.n: The bare-Ὅτι moral formula again (as in the Golden Eggs). κοινωνεῖν, 'to go shares,' answers κοινωνός in the lion's speech — partnership is exactly the thing the moral warns you off.
